draft.introduction: The Mūlapariyāya Sutta is the Buddha’s analysis of how an unenlightened person misperceives reality by taking things as “mine.” The arahant directly knows without appropriation.

translation.text: The Blessed One said: “I will teach you the root of all things. An untaught ordinary person perceives earth as earth. Having perceived earth as earth, he conceives himself as earth, conceives himself in earth, conceives earth to be ‘mine.’”
translation.original_text: evaṃ me sutaṃ | bhagavā ukkaṭṭhāya viharati subhagavane

translation.text: But a bhikkhu who is an arahant directly knows earth as earth. Having directly known earth as earth, he does not conceive himself as earth. Why? Because he has fully understood it.
translation.original_text: yo pi so bhikkhu arahaṃ khīṇāsavo | so paṭhavīṃ paṭhavīto abhijānāti

translator_notes.note: The distinction between “perceives” (sañjānāti) and “directly knows” (abhijānāti) is central. Ordinary perception involves conceiving—projecting self onto objects. The arahant perceives but does not conceive; he knows without appropriating.
